does: This paper conducts a systematic empirical audit of the Agentverse platform, a production agent-native cloud infrastructure operated by Fetch.ai. The authors catalog 204 API endpoints as of Q1 2026 and characterize their operational status, deployment stage, and gaps. They derive a Gap Taxonomy organizing 62 distinct missing capabilities across eight categories including agent memory, observability, security, economic primitives, and enterprise scaling. The work establishes a baseline inventory of current agent infrastructure capabilities and rigorously documents where autonomous systems currently lack essential support for reliable, production-scale deployment.
connects: This work extends research in autonomous agent systems and distributed computing infrastructure by shifting focus from agent cognition and reasoning—which has dominated recent literature—toward the foundational platform requirements agents need. It builds on earlier work on multi-agent systems, agent communication protocols, and decentralized computing, while responding to the practical realization that production deployment of agents at scale reveals critical infrastructure gaps overlooked by capability-focused research. The paper connects to broader efforts in the AI systems community around robustness, observability, and economic coordination in distributed settings, grounding these abstract concerns in concrete platform engineering constraints.
enables: By systematically documenting infrastructure gaps, this work establishes a roadmap for building next-generation agent platforms capable of supporting reliable autonomous systems at enterprise scale. It enables future infrastructure research to prioritize high-impact capabilities based on deployment maturity data. The taxonomy framework itself becomes a reusable tool for evaluating and designing agent platforms, potentially accelerating the path from experimental agent research to production systems. The findings also inform standards development efforts and guide investment in critical missing pieces like persistent agent memory systems, comprehensive observability tooling, secure agent-to-agent interaction models, and formal economic mechanisms for resource allocation and agent compensation.
